title: Fitting electric poultry netting
subtitle: Setting up and energising a 50-metre roll of electric mesh for a free-range flock.
excerpt: Electric poultry netting is the fastest way to secure a rotating outdoor run for backyard chickens. It takes around twenty minutes to lay out and stakes in without tools. Energised and on dry ground it is effective against foxes; the details that make it reliable are in the soil contact, the tensioning, and the energiser-to-perimeter match.
sourceNotes:
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Laying Hens (2002), predator protection section. https://www.gov.uk
National Farmers Union small-flock predator management guidance.

content.text: A standard 50-metre roll of electric poultry netting is 112 cm tall with the bottom strand connected to the

via the active clips and the bottom line earthed into the soil. The top horizontal strands carry the charge; a fox's nose or paw touching the mesh receives a pulse that is startling but not injurious. A fox that has tested it once rarely tests it again.

Clear vegetation from the fence line before laying out the net: grass and weeds touching the lower strands leach the charge to earth and can reduce the voltage at nose-contact below the deterrent threshold. Carry the rolled net to the first corner and push in the first stake by foot pressure, angled slightly outwards from the enclosure. Unroll the net while walking the perimeter, pushing in each integral step-in post as you go. Space the posts so the mesh stands upright without sagging: for most soils, the pre-spaced posts do this automatically. Corner posts need the integral tensioning supports pushed in to prevent the net folding inwards.

Connect the energiser's live terminal to the netting's active clip and the earth terminal to a ground stake driven at least 50 cm into the soil beside the energiser. Do not rely on the netting's bottom strand as the only earth: a separate driven earth stake gives the system a reliable return path. Switch the energiser on and use a voltage tester to check the fence is carrying at least 3,000 volts around the full perimeter. A reading below 2,000 volts at any point means there is a fault, probably vegetation contact or a poor earth connection.

Switch off the energiser before removing or repositioning any section of net. Touching a live electric fence with both hands, especially in wet conditions, delivers a more severe shock than a nose contact and can cause muscle spasm in adults. Teach anyone who helps with the chickens to turn the energiser off before handling the fence.
